sanskrit_devanagari:
यद्यदाचरति श्रेष्ठस्तत्तदेवेतरो जनः ।
स यत्प्रमाणं कुरुते लोकस्तदनुवर्तते ॥ २१ ॥
sanskrit_iast:
yad yad ācarati śreṣṭhas
tat tad evetaro janaḥ |
sa yat pramāṇaṁ kurute
lokas tad anuvartate || 21 ||

exact (academic critical edition; cross-reference Bhg_03.021 = MBh_06,025.021). Body text identical; word-segmentation differs by edition.

translations.verbatim_quote: Whatever action a great man performs, common men follow. And whatever standards he sets by exemplary acts, all the world pursues.
(See holy-bhagavad-gita.org for full Mukundananda rendering; agreement with Prabhupada noted in disagreements section where it diverges materially.)

commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: People in general always require a leader who can teach the public by practical behavior. A leader cannot teach the public to stop smoking if he himself smokes. Lord Caitanya said that a teacher should behave properly before he begins teaching. One who teaches in that way is called ācārya, or the ideal teacher.

commentaries.translator: Swami Gambhirananda (Advaita Ashrama edition of Sankara-bhasya, as reproduced on bhagavad-gita.us)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: 3.21 Yat yat, [This is according to the Ast. The G1. Pr. reads, yat yat yesu yesu.-Tr.] whatever action; a sresthah, superior person, a leader; acarati, does; itarah, another; janah, person, who follows him; does tat tat eva, that very action.
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: Shankara: yat yat — whatever — the śreṣṭha (the leading person, the knower of scripture and right practice) does, that very thing the other person, the follower, does. And whatever pramāṇa (authority — Vedic or secular) he upholds by his own conduct, the populace adopts as their pramāṇa. The verse therefore charges the śreṣṭha with a role he cannot decline: his behavior is the populace's authority, whether he intends it or not.

commentaries.translator: Swami Adidevananda (Sri Ramakrishna Math edition of Ramanuja's Gita-bhasya, as reproduced on bhagavad-gita.us)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: 3.21 Whatever ‘an eminent man,’ i.e., he, who is famous for his knowledge of all the scriptures and for his observance of the scriptural dictates, performs, others who have incomplete knowledge of the scriptures will also perform, following his example.
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: Ramanuja: 'an eminent man' — he who is famous for his knowledge of all the scriptures and for his observance of the scriptural dictates — whatever he performs, others who have incomplete knowledge of the scriptures will also perform, following his example. The pramāṇa he establishes through ancillary practice (saparikara, the act with all its limbs) is what the watcher adopts as the binding standard.

positions.rendering: Whatever action a great man performs... whatever standards he sets by exemplary acts, all the world pursues
Whatever actions great persons perform, common people follow. Whatever standards they set by their exemplary acts, all the world pursues
śreṣṭha = the leading person, knower of the scriptures; pramāṇa = the authority (Vedic or worldly) that he upholds; people imitate both his acts and the authority he treats as binding

śreṣṭha = the eminent personage known for scriptural knowledge and observance; pramāṇa = the standard he sets by ancillary practice; ordinary people with incomplete knowledge follow what he visibly does

disagreements_among_translators.explanation: Both traditions read śreṣṭha narrowly — not 'any famous person' but 'the one with established knowledge and right practice' — and both read pramāṇa as the standard implicitly authorised by his behavior. Translators broaden śreṣṭha to 'a great man' or 'great persons,' which loses the verse's specificity. The verse is naming a particular kind of person and a particular function (his acts become the operating standard for those without his knowledge); collapsing this to 'leaders' is a real loss of doctrinal precision.

literal_meaning: Whatever a great person does, others do as well. Whatever standard he establishes, the people follow.
traditional_meaning_consensus: One of the chapter's most-quoted verses. Krishna names the structural fact: the behavior of the śreṣṭha (the eminent, the leader) becomes the populace's pramāṇa (standard, authoritative measure). The verse is descriptive, not aspirational — it is naming a load-bearing claim about how the social order is shaped, which sets up Krishna's own example at 3.22-3.24.
